summary: O'Neil argues that smart, feminist women often abandon their critical thinking skills when they become pregnant, deferring excessively to doctors on matters they're perfectly capable of deciding themselves. She attributes this to pregnancy hormones, high stakes, and fear-inducing pregnancy literature. The post pushes back against medical paternalism in pregnancy care, noting that evidence for many prescriptive guidelines is weak. She concludes that women should apply the same skepticism to pregnancy advice that they would to any other paternalistic nonsense, remembering that their own wellbeing matters alongside their baby's.
key_points: Pregnancy shouldn't mean surrendering decades of competent self-care to doctors who dictate eating and pain management
Much pregnancy advice lacks solid evidence but gets treated as medical gospel
Childbirth is safer than ever, making fear-based compliance less justified
Medical paternalism doesn't stop being paternalism when the doctor is a woman
Maternal wellbeing matters too—happy mothers make for fine babies
money_quotes: And then they get pregnant and the lose most if not all of those properties.
They get doctors who tell them what to eat, and how much, even though they've been doing quite well feeding themselves for 30 odd years without help.
Plus there's all those ridiculous and scary pregnancy books out there which just serve to make women neurotic and should be burned.
But here's the thing, there's not much evidence about exactly how you should eat when you're pregnant, unless you are doing something absolutely weird.
So if you have one of those pushy-ass doctors, fuck 'em. This is your body, you get to decide that stuff.
For goodness sakes don't forget your feminist training.
It's not just your baby here, it's also you, and yes you deserve to eat food you want to eat and to moderate pain if it gets overwhelming.
key_insight: Pregnancy doesn't revoke your feminist credentials or your right to question medical authority about your own body.
